content:
11 min read
Just now

--

“Give the agent access to the terminal and the file system, and it will handle everything” sounds great right up until the moment the agent edits a production config instead of a test file, or overwrites a file full of API keys because it “was in the way” of the task. That’s usually when someone on the team, often security or a tech lead, asks the obvious question: how do we actually control what this agent does to our code?
The problem is that “controlling an agent” is not a single switch in the settings. It’s a set of independent mechanisms, each closing off one concrete failure mode: the agent reads secrets, the agent bends a test to match a bug, the agent touches the wrong module, the agent edits a production config with no diff to review. Below are six levels of control, each with the scenario it exists to prevent and how it’s implemented in Explyt, a plugin for JetBrains IDEs.
Level 1: what the agent can see and where it can write
How do I stop the agent from reading my secrets in .env?
That’s what .readignore is for: a file in .explyt/tools/ using .gitignore syntax. If a path is listed there, the agent never sees the file at all, not for reading, not for writing. It simply never enters its context.
# .explyt/tools/.readignore
.env
secrets/**How do I protect deployment configs and the agent’s own instructions from an accidental edit?
That’s what .writeignore is for: same .gitignore syntax, different effect. If a path is listed there, the agent can read the file but can't edit it. This is the right fit for files the agent benefits from seeing for context but shouldn't touch: docker-compose.yml, Kubernetes manifests, or AGENTS.md itself, which the agent shouldn't be able to rewrite.
# .explyt/tools/.writeignore
docker-compose.yml
k8s/**
AGENTS.mdHow do I stop the agent from bending a test to match a bug instead of fixing the actual logic?
A classic failure mode: the agent is fixing a bug, can’t work out the logic within a reasonable number of attempts, and instead changes the assertion in the test itself so it turns green. Edit Scope restricts not individual files but the whole editing surface at once: the agent sees the entire project, but can propose and apply changes only inside the files and folders you’ve attached to the task. If you attach only the module’s source code and leave the tests out, the agent physically can’t edit the test to dodge the check. It can only read it for context. This is exactly what TDD needs: code can change, tests can’t, so the agent is forced to fix the cause instead of the symptom. The difference from .writeignore is scope: Edit Scope is convenient to turn on for a single task ("only touch module X for this task, don't attach the tests"), while .readignore and .writeignore define a standing project policy that can be committed and shared with the team.
By default, the agent is confined to the project’s root directory and doesn’t read arbitrary files elsewhere on disk.
Level 2: how the agent understands the boundaries of a task
The file-level restrictions from Level 1 physically close off access. But a good part of what the agent does or doesn’t do isn’t decided by a locked path. It’s decided by an instruction the agent sees in the system prompt.
How do I get the agent to follow one test style, say, only write tests in given-when-then?
That’s what Rules are for: a Markdown fragment with a glob pattern that applies whenever the currently open file matches it. A rule can lock in a specific style: write tests only in given-when-then, name variables by one convention, follow a specific pattern for new services. Rules can be kept locally, just for you, or checked into the repository, so the whole team works from the same recommendations.
How do I stop re-explaining the project structure to the agent in every new chat?
That’s what AGENTS.md is for: an open file-format standard in the project root that describes the domain, the repository layout, and what shouldn't be touched without confirmation. It doesn't replace Rules; it gives the agent shared context so nobody has to describe the project from scratch in every chat.
Rules and AGENTS.md don't work like a firewall. The model sees their content as text in the system prompt and weighs it alongside every other signal, instead of being forced to follow it. Only the file-level mechanisms from Level 1 provide a hard guarantee.
Level 3: how much autonomy a mode grants
How do I make sure the agent in a code-review mode won’t edit a file by accident?
That’s what the choice of working mode is for: some modes simply have no tools for changing code.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Data from the Explyt feature matrix, “Working modes” section.
Ask runs in read-only mode: the agent can search, read, and explain, but has no access to the terminal and no tool for editing a file. Review, per the table above, also doesn’t edit files and doesn’t run the debugger, but unlike Ask it keeps terminal and build access, and it’s the only mode with access to IDE inspections. General is the only mode besides Orchestrator that can call sub-agents.
How do I avoid paying for a top-tier model when I’m asking the agent for a one-line fix?
That’s what sub-agent profiles (Model Routing) are for: you can pin a specific model to a specific type of subtask. A small fix, a code search, or reading files doesn’t need the same model as a hard refactor. Cheap, fast subtasks can run on a cheap, fast model, while refactoring and deep review are better handled by a model with stronger code understanding. This is also a form of control, just not over what the agent can touch, but over which model makes the decisions in each subtask and how much that costs.
Level 4: what happens afterward
Even if the agent worked fully autonomously, the last word still belongs to a human.
How do I check exactly what the agent changed before it lands in a commit?
That’s what Agent Changes is for: a window similar to a pre-commit change list, with the files from the current chat on the left and the diff for the selected file on the right. You can accept a change in full, reject part of it, or go file by file. Changes from different chats never mix.
How do I catch a SQL injection the agent missed in its own diff?
That’s what a dedicated static analysis engine (SAST) is for. It runs on compiled sources and traces the data path from a source, say, an HTTP request parameter, to a dangerous sink, say, a SQL query, across the whole project rather than a single function. The analysis runs offline, without sending code anywhere, and currently supports Java and Kotlin in IntelliJ IDEA and Android Studio.
Findings land in a dedicated Review window alongside the results of any other manual check: a list of findings tied to a file and line, with a “Fix with agent” button on each one.
The final call still belongs to a human in the Agent Changes window, but thanks to the inspections, the Review agent has more to lean on than just what the model happened to notice in the diff.
Level 5: extensions are under control too
How do I make sure code isn’t leaking to a model vendor’s servers outside my control?
MCP servers, an open protocol for connecting an LLM to external services, give the agent access to outside tools: GitHub, issue trackers, search engines, and so on.
How do I avoid carrying a GitHub MCP server’s token into a project that doesn’t need it?
MCP server configuration can be scoped to a specific project (.explyt/mcp_servers.json) instead of applying globally to every project at once.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How do I avoid storing an MCP server’s token in plain text in a config file that’s checked into the repository?
Tokens and keys in the config don’t have to sit in plain text. They can be pulled from environment variables through the ${env:VARIABLE_NAME} placeholder.
How do I make the agent stronger by giving it more IDE tools?
Once connected, the agent gets the IDE’s own additional tools and uses them alongside the rest of the MCP tools.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What do I do if the agent stops working after connecting a couple of MCP servers?
The chat used to have a hard limit of 128 tools. Large MCP servers expose dozens of tools each: GitHub, for example, offers around 70 (read an issue, read a PR, check notifications, star a repo, and so on). Connecting just two or three such servers was enough to hit the limit and break the agent.
Now, if the combined number of tools exceeds 128, each MCP server gets wrapped into its own sub-agent. The main agent only sees one tool for talking to that server, while the full set of commands stays inside the sub-agent. If a server is small (DuckDuckGo, for example, with just 2 tools) and the overall limit isn’t exceeded, it keeps working directly, without spinning up a sub-agent. There’s nothing to configure: the plugin decides on its own when to use a sub-agent and when to call the server directly.
While the call is running, an expandable panel appears in the chat: Running <mcp_server>. Inside, a Progress section shows which tools the sub-agent is using, and once the call finishes, the result appears in a Finished block.
Level 6: where the code goes and where the model runs
How do I make sure code isn’t leaving my control before it even reaches a model?
Everything above controls what the agent does inside the project. But there’s another question security teams tend to ask before any of the others: where does the code from the IDE actually go once the agent assembles context for a model request? There are three options here, and they’re determined by the plan and settings, not hardcoded into the agent:

• Paid subscription or a flex pack (per-minute billing for access to top models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google): code and prompt travel over a controlled channel to the chosen model provider, with no training on user data.

• BYOK, your own provider API key (available on the free Community plan too): data goes directly to the provider you chose, under that provider’s own policy, bypassing Explyt’s servers entirely.

• Local and on-premise models (inside your company’s perimeter, over a VPC, or a full on-premise deployment): code never leaves your company’s infrastructure at all.
On any paid plan, the customer picks the model provider, instead of the vendor deciding for them. Enterprise customers get dedicated deployment options: on-premise, a private cloud (VPC), or self-hosted, with a specific configuration and model matched to their infrastructure and security requirements.
This connects back to the file-level controls through Edit Scope from Level 1: if certain files or folders need to be excluded from what goes to the model, you can restrict what the agent even sees well before you ever choose the delivery channel for that code.
Where this works: the feature matrix across IDEs
None of the mechanisms described above is exclusive to IntelliJ IDEA. According to the official Explyt feature matrix, nearly everything in this article works the same way across all seven supported JetBrains IDEs:
Control mechanismIDEAAndroid
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One row needs a separate note. Static vulnerability scanning (SAST) relies on analyzing compiled Java/Kotlin sources, so it’s only available in IntelliJ IDEA and Android Studio. PyCharm, GoLand, Rider, WebStorm, and PhpStorm don’t have this layer of control at all, and it shouldn’t be counted on there. The other six levels of control covered in this article (file restrictions, Rules, AGENTS.md, modes, Agent Changes, MCP) are available across all these IDEs: the base control mechanisms aren’t tied to a specific project language.
What control doesn’t guarantee
Being honest about where the limits of this toolkit sit:

• Rules and AGENTS.md are a recommendation to the agent, not a hard rule (see Level 2). The model can miss them or drift from them under the pull of oth
